content:
OVERVIEW

Academic SOC rotation at PISCES International through Central Washington's IT program, Spring 2024. The rotation put students next to a working SOC that monitored thirteen municipal network feeds on the ELK stack. My hands-on work was dashboard building and first-line ticketing against what the dashboards surfaced — not detection authoring, not shift ownership, not stakeholder briefing.

WHAT THE WORK ACTUALLY WAS

Built a handful of Kibana visualizations against the environment's log indices: alert volume over time, custom anomaly panels, time-series breakdowns, bar charts keyed to municipality. I used my own dashboards as a first-pass lens and wrote MantisBT tickets on whatever looked irregular — varying severity, no escalation authority past ticket creation. The analysts who triaged alerts at scale were doing something different from what I was doing; I got exposure to how their shift worked, not experience running one.

WHAT THE ROTATION TAUGHT

Observability work sits upstream of detection work. A good dashboard makes a weak analyst useful; a bad dashboard makes a good analyst slow. I don't own detections now — I own making the signal visible before someone else has to hunt for it. That stance is what I took from PISCES, not a skill. The skill is what I'm still building.
